Text:
"Give sux — it wears off fast if I can't intubate." Sugammadex quietly rewrote that rule.

🔖 Save this for your RSI reasoning: spontaneous sux recovery (~8–10 min) can outlast the patient's oxygen, but sugammadex 16 mg/kg reverses profound rocuronium block in ~2–3 min — faster, and without hyperkalemia, MH risk, or bradycardia.

Text:
Obese patients desaturate in seconds even after a flawless preox — and the reason is physics, not technique. 🫁 Low FRC (shrunken O2 reservoir) + high oxygen consumption (fast drain) = almost no apneic buffer. Supine makes both worse; ramping (HELP position: ear to sternal notch, head elevated) unloads the diaphragm, restores FRC, and improves your laryngoscopic view. Add head-up preox + nasal-cannula apneic oxygenation.

Text:
Three aspiration meds. Only one protects the patient you're inducing right now.

Sodium citrate (non-particulate antacid) neutralizes the acid already sitting in the stomach within minutes — so it's the one that matters for the emergent, full-stomach case. Famotidine only blocks NEW acid secretion and needs 30–60+ minutes. Metoclopramide needs lead time too, and opioids or mechanical obstruction blunt it. Give the citrate LAST, right before induction. And never reach for a particulate antacid — it's the one that actually damages lung if aspirated.
